hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, the property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. C

photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Facade is occupied and readable from the street, with contemporary storefront alterations layered onto the earlier building form.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Simple fascia and coping with limited decorative trim visible.
photo_observations.chimney_details: No chimney clearly visible from the selected frontage view.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Character comes primarily from surviving massing, cladding, and current storefront treatment.
photo_observations.storefront_description: Two salon-style storefronts with dark glazing and a fenced sidewalk patio/platform.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Storefront and signage have been updated from the original historic configuration.
photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: light cream brick facade with an orange nail studio sign and dark storefront glazing
photo_observations.window_details: Regularly spaced upper windows above two ground-floor salon/retail bays and side entrance doors.
photo_observations.door_details: One shared-looking central stair/lobby entry plus separate storefront entries for the salon units.
photo_observations.signage_observed: Nail Studio sign and salon window graphics.

storefront_description: Two salon-style storefronts with dark glazing and a fenced sidewalk patio/platform.

facade_description: 151 Augusta Ave presents a row-house facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its house-form, row typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 4, 4, 0 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 3 principal entrances. Character-defining facade elements include cornice. HCD note: The property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, the property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. C
heritage_summary: C. The property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles.
